name: Poukai
description: The Poukai is a monstrous bird in Māori mythology, a giant eagle-like creature said to inhabit mountainous regions, particularly the Southern Alps of New Zealand. It is described as having immense size, capable of carrying off humans and livestock as prey. Tales of the Poukai often depict it as a fearsome predator, a symbol of power and danger in the wilderness. Its association with mountains and remote areas reinforces its image as a creature beyond human control, a force of nature to be reckoned with. Oral traditions vary, with some accounts portraying the Poukai as a purely malevolent being, while others suggest a more complex relationship with the environment and other creatures. Regardless, the Poukai remains a significant figure in Māori folklore, embodying the wild and untamed aspects of the natural world and serving as a reminder of the power and unpredictability of the mountains. The Poukai's cry is said to be a terrifying shriek that echoes through the valleys, warning of its presence. It is often associated with storms and other turbulent weather conditions, further solidifying its connection to the forces of nature. Some stories link the Poukai to the legendary giant, Te Rapuwai, suggesting a connection between these mythical beings and the ancient history of the land. The Poukai's presence in Māori narratives serves as a cautionary tale, highlighting the importance of respecting the environment and acknowledging the power of the natural world. Its image continues to resonate in contemporary Māori art and culture, representing both the beauty and the danger of the New Zealand landscape.
subtitle: Giant Man-Eating Eagle of the Mountains
appearance: The Poukai is described as an enormous bird, resembling an eagle but much larger. Its wingspan is said to be immense, casting a shadow over entire valleys. Its feathers are dark and glossy, with sharp, powerful talons capable of tearing flesh. The Poukai has a piercing gaze and a hooked beak, perfectly adapted for hunting and devouring prey.
habitat: The Poukai is said to inhabit mountainous regions, particularly the Southern Alps of New Zealand. It nests on high peaks and cliffs, where it can survey its territory and launch attacks on unsuspecting prey. The Poukai prefers remote and inaccessible areas, far from human settlements.
behavior: The Poukai is a solitary and territorial creature, fiercely protective of its domain. It is a formidable predator, preying on humans, livestock, and other large animals. The Poukai is known for its aggressive behavior and its terrifying cry, which serves as a warning to those who dare to trespass on its territory. It is often associated with storms and turbulent weather.
weaknesses: Vulnerable to attacks on its nest

keyMyths.title: Poukai and Te Rapuwai
keyMyths.description: Some stories link the Poukai to Te Rapuwai, a legendary giant. This connection suggests an ancient lineage and a shared history between these mythical beings.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell
sources.work: The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Māori mythology and folklore.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Māori mythology and folklore.
